topics.evidenceSummary: Body weight and composition regulated by intake, expenditure, appetite, satiety and behaviour in a multifactorial system.
keyMechanisms: Energy intake and expenditure interact with appetite signals
Sedentary behaviour and low activity increase risk
Sleep, adherence and medications modify behaviour
appImplications: Do not infer causality from one metric
Use multi-signal coaching: intake, activity, sleep, waist, performance, adherence
Explain obesity risk as multifactorial

topics.evidenceSummary: Fat loss and muscle gain can occur together in beginners, detrained users, or those with higher adiposity; less predictable in lean highly trained users.
keyMechanisms: Resistance training increases muscle protein synthesis
Adequate protein helps preserve fat-free mass during deficits
Modest deficits sustain better than aggressive cuts
appImplications: Default new users to recomp mode before hard cut/bulk
Judge progress with waist, photos, strength and weight trend
Avoid promising simultaneous rapid fat loss and muscle gain

topics.evidenceSummary: Resistance training improves strength and hypertrophy across formats; program variables adjusted to target outcome and user capacity.
keyMechanisms: Frequency, intensity, volume and range shape adaptation
Strength responds to heavier loading and 2+ sessions/week
Hypertrophy depends on adequate weekly volume
appImplications: Program builder should prescribe progressive overload
Track sets per muscle group not just workout completion
Support gym, home, bands and bodyweight

topics.evidenceSummary: Total daily protein matters more than narrow timing windows; distributing protein across meals is reasonable but total adequacy drives outcomes.
keyMechanisms: Protein supports muscle protein synthesis and lean-mass retention
Resistance training sensitizes muscle to amino acids
Meal-level distribution is secondary optimization after total intake
appImplications: Give protein-first nudges before supplement suggestions
Encourage 3-5 protein occasions rather than one huge serving
Flag low-protein days as higher priority than imperfect timing

topics.topic: Energy cycling, intermittent restriction, refeeds and adaptive thermogenesis
topics.evidenceSummary: Intermittent and continuous restriction both work; evidence doesn't justify forcing refeeds/diet breaks on everyone, but behavioral and fatigue benefits useful in selected users.
keyMechanisms: Energy restriction can reduce resting expenditure and NEAT
Fatigue and hunger degrade adherence
Planned maintenance periods may improve sustainability
appImplications: Offer diet breaks as optional adherence tool not magic reset
When adherence high but performance/steps/mood drop, consider maintenance week
Use plateau logic only after confirming logging quality and trend stability

topics.evidenceSummary: Adults generally need at least 7 hours sleep; supports mood, energy, glucose control, recovery and adherence.
keyMechanisms: Inadequate sleep worsens recovery and decision quality
Poor sleep interacts with stress and appetite regulation
Training progression without recovery increases relapse risk
appImplications: Do not escalate calories-down and training-up when sleep poor
Show recovery warnings before pushing intensity
Use sleep trends in plateau analysis

topics.topic: Menstrual cycle, low energy availability and pregnancy
topics.evidenceSummary: Symptom tracking is more defensible than rigid phase-based prescriptions. Exercise generally safe in uncomplicated pregnancy. Persistent menstrual disturbance in active users triggers LEA review.
keyMechanisms: Low energy availability impairs reproductive and bone health
Pregnancy raises nutrient needs and changes contraindications
Cycle symptoms affect readiness and adherence
appImplications: Track symptoms, bleeding, cramps, energy and cycle regularity
Escalate amenorrhoea/oligomenorrhoea to review/referral
Use pregnancy-safe coaching mode with clinician flag

topics.evidenceSummary: Older adults benefit strongly from resistance training, protein sufficiency, balance work and functional progression; weight-loss advice more conservative when muscle loss risk high.
keyMechanisms: Ageing increases muscle-loss risk and lowers reserve
Strength and function respond to training even in older adults
Protein and resistance together improve physical function
appImplications: Prefer strength-preserving fat-loss plans over aggressive cuts
Track chair-rise, grip or rep-quality proxies
Include balance and mobility blocks for older users

topics.evidenceSummary: Chronic conditions and medications materially change weight, appetite, exercise tolerance, glucose risk and nutrition guidance.
keyMechanisms: Diabetes meds alter glycaemic risk around exercise and meals
Some widely used medications promote weight gain
CKD, hypertension and pregnancy alter protein, sodium and hydration logic
appImplications: Collect medication classes not just free text
Require clinician check prompts for insulin/sulfonylureas, pregnancy, CKD
Suppress extreme macro/calorie coaching in high-risk groups

topics.topic: Hydration, electrolytes, micronutrients and gut-health basics
topics.evidenceSummary: Hydration and electrolyte needs are context-dependent; sodium/potassium guidance matters for NCD risk; fiber and minimally processed foods support gut and metabolic health.
keyMechanisms: Hydration status changes with losses, environment, workload
Higher sodium/lower potassium patterns worsen cardiometabolic risk
Fiber and minimally processed foods shape microbiome and energy balance
Micronutrient inadequacy impairs performance, mood and pregnancy outcomes
appImplications: Coach hydration by context not universal target
Give food-first micronutrient prompts before supplements
Flag low-fibre days and repeated GI symptoms for gradual adjustments

topics.topic: Behavioural adherence, relapse prevention and measurement best practice
topics.evidenceSummary: Self-monitoring, support and habit formation improve adherence; metrics should be framed as trend signals with uncertainty rather than exact truths.
keyMechanisms: Tracking increases awareness and allows earlier correction
Habits reduce friction more effectively than repeated motivation
Single measurements are noisier than multi-week trends
appImplications: Use streaks for behaviours not just scale changes
Prefer 7-28 day rolling trends over daily interpretation
Expose confidence badge for each nutrition and body-composition estimate
